nativeRange.habitat: Most reef environments, with a preference for lagoons and subtidal rocky substrates; also found on upper reef slopes; colonies tend to be uncommon but conspicuous when present

decorPreferences: open spacing from neighboring corals (minimum 10–15 cm clearance)
indirect moderate flow — avoid direct powerhead blasting

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target-feeding once or twice per week with small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, reef roids, or similar coral blended foods — tends to support growth and coloration. Polyps generally expand at night, making evening feeding sessions more effective. Well-lit, stable systems can sustain the coral on photosynthesis alone, but regular feeding is broadly recommended.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (5–10 cm)
aggressive LPS corals (Galaxea, Hammer, Torch) without generous spacing
fish known to nip at LPS polyps (some butterflyfish)

summary: The Oulophyllia Coral is an uncommon large polyp stony (LPS) brain coral with broad, deep valleys that give it a more open, maze-like appearance than closely related species such as Platygyra. It is generally considered an intermediate-level coral, doing best with moderate lighting, moderate water movement, and stable reef water chemistry.
careNotes:
Oulophyllia is a genus of three described species — O. crispa (most commonly encountered in the aquarium trade), O. bennettae, and O. levis.

Oulophyllia corals are zooxanthellate (meaning they host symbiotic algae that produce much of their energy through photosynthesis), large polyp stony corals that form submassive to hemispherical domed colonies. Their most distinctive feature is a pattern of broad, V-shaped winding channels — sometimes called meandroid valleys — reported up to 20 mm wide, separated by thin, sharp-edged ridges. The overall appearance closely resembles a brain or maze, similar to Platygyra but with noticeably wider, deeper channels. Wild colonies have been reported frequently exceeding one metre in diameter, though aquarium specimens and Red Sea populations are typically smaller, often 15–40 cm in a home reef setting. O. bennettae tends to be greenish-grey with green oral discs; O. crispa is typically uniform grey, or shows brown ridges with pale cream or pink channel floors. Polyps expand primarily at night.

Oulophyllia is uncommon in the wild and correspondingly uncommon in the aquarium trade, tending to appear through specialty coral vendors or live auction listings rather than as a standard catalog item.

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Moderate intensity is generally advised, broadly comparable to other similarly structured LPS corals in the family Merulinidae such as Platygyra and Goniastrea. A PAR range of 75–200 is typical; newly acquired specimens should be acclimated gradually over 2–4 weeks, starting lower in the aquarium and moving upward to prevent bleaching.
• Flow: Moderate, indirect, random-pattern flow tends to work well. Sufficient movement to remove detritus and deliver food is beneficial; directing a powerhead blast at the colony should generally be avoided as it can stress the tissue.
• Placement: Middle rockwork or a stable sandbed is broadly recommended. At least 10–15 cm of clear space from neighboring corals is advisable to account for sweeper tentacle reach.
• Aggression: Oulophyllia have been observed extending sweeper tentacles — generally in the 5–10 cm range — particularly at night and when food is sensed. These tentacles can sting neighboring corals. Overall inter-coral aggression is considered moderate; standard LPS spacing precautions are advisable.
• Water chemistry: Standard reef parameters are broadly recommended. Stable alkalinity (8–10 dKH), calcium (390–450 ppm), and moderate magnesium (1250–1350 ppm) are typical targets. Relatively low nitrate (under ~20 ppm) and low phosphate are preferred, though some nutrient availability is thought to support coloration.
• Bleaching: Oulophyllia corals tend to be susceptible to bleaching if lighting conditions change abruptly or water quality declines. Standard LPS quarantine and dipping protocols are advisable on new acquisitions.
• Nocturnal polyp extension: Polyps tend to expand primarily at night; daytime appearance may not reflect full colony health.
breedingNotes: Oulophyllia can be propagated through fragging — cutting a colony into smaller pieces so each fragment grows into a new coral — but this tends to be slower and more challenging than fragging branching LPS corals. Because the colony grows as a solid dome rather than as branches, each cut must preserve enough skeleton with intact tissue to support regrowth. In the wild, Oulophyllia reproduces sexually through broadcast spawning, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. Captive sexual reproduction is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: Wikipedia – Oulophyllia crispa
primary.notes: Used for taxonomy (Merulinidae / Scleractinia), native range (Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, East Africa to Australia, Japan, East China Sea, West Pacific), habitat (lagoons, subtidal rocks, to ~30 m depth), colony size (frequently >1 m across; Red Sea populations rarely >20 cm), coloration, and morphology. World Wide Corals (primary slice source) had no Oulophyllia page; Wikipedia used as effective primary source.

additional.name: Corals of the World – Oulophyllia crispa factsheet
additional.notes: Cross-check source. Confirmed: genus Oulophyllia Milne Edwards & Haime 1848; broad V-shaped valleys (up to 20 mm); submassive or hemispherical colony form; colonies frequently over 1 m across; habitat 'most reef environments, especially in lagoons'; abundance 'uncommon'. O. bennettae confirmed as separate species (upper reef slopes habitat). BRS and LiveAquaria Oulophyllia-specific pages both 404'd; this served as primary secondary.

additional.name: WoRMS – Oulophyllia (AphiaID 205663)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation: Family Merulinidae, Order Scleractinia, AphiaID 205663. Distribution records confirm: Indian Ocean, West Pacific, Central Pacific, South Asia, Southeast Asia, East Asia, Australasia, Melanesia, Micronesia. No husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Oulophyllia (genus)
additional.notes: Confirmed three species in genus (O. crispa, O. bennettae, O. levis); massive colonies with broad ridges between corallites; tropical western and central Indo-Pacific range. Used for taxonomy and alsoKnownAs scope.
